ai.error.no_key: Kimi API Key not configured. Add KIMI_API_KEY=sk-xxx to your .env file.
ai.pka.acid_desc: Tends to donate protons (H⁺). Unionized in stomach (pH≈1.5) for good absorption, ionized in blood (pH≈7.4) for excretion.
ai.pka.amphoteric_desc: Can donate or accept protons depending on pH. Absorption is complex and varies with administration site pH.
ai.pka.base_desc: Tends to accept protons (H⁺). Ionized in stomach, mainly absorbed in small intestine (pH≈6.8), ionized in blood.
ai.shap.title: 【SHAP Key Features】(Top 3 by importance)
ai.shap.unavailable: (SHAP data unavailable, section 4 will be skipped)
analysis.admet.absorption.hba_high: H-Bond Acceptors > 10, excessive H-bond acceptors reduce membrane permeability
analysis.admet.absorption.hbd_high: H-Bond Donors > 5, desolvation required for membrane crossing, may reduce absorption
analysis.admet.absorption.pka_acid: pKa = {val:.1f} (acidic), unionized in stomach (pH 1.5), good gastric absorption
analysis.admet.absorption.pka_base: pKa = {val:.1f} (basic), ionized in stomach, mainly absorbed in small intestine (pH 6.8)
analysis.admet.absorption.pka_neutral: pKa = {val:.1f} (near neutral), absorbed throughout GI tract
analysis.admet.absorption.tpsa_high: TPSA > 140 Å², large polar surface may limit passive trans-membrane absorption
analysis.admet.absorption.tpsa_low: TPSA < 60 Å², good intestinal membrane permeability
analysis.admet.distribution.bbb: Low TPSA + moderate LogP = may cross Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB)
analysis.admet.distribution.hydrophilic: Highly hydrophilic (LogP < 0), mainly distributed in plasma and extracellular fluid
analysis.admet.distribution.lipophilic: Highly lipophilic (LogP > 3),倾向于 distribute to adipose tissue and cross BBB
analysis.admet.excretion.conjugate: Carboxyl/phenolic OH →容易 form Phase II conjugates for renal excretion
analysis.admet.excretion.hepato: LogP > 3 →容易 reabsorbed in renal tubules, increased hepatobiliary excretion
analysis.admet.excretion.renal: Low MW + moderate hydrophilicity → renal excretion (glomerular filtration)
analysis.admet.excretion.tpsa: High TPSA favors renal excretion (water-soluble metabolites)
analysis.admet.metabolism.amide: Amide bond (metabolically stable, but may be cleaved by amidases)
analysis.admet.metabolism.amine_sec: Primary/secondary amine (may undergo N-dealkylation or N-oxidation)
analysis.admet.metabolism.aromatic: Aromatic rings (may undergo CYP450 oxidation to epoxides/phenols)
analysis.admet.metabolism.carboxylic: Carboxyl group (may undergo amino acid conjugation or acyl glucuronidation)
analysis.admet.metabolism.ester: Ester bond (hydrolyzed by esterases, may have significant first-pass effect)
analysis.admet.metabolism.fallback: Simple structure, primary metabolic pathways require experimental validation
analysis.admet.metabolism.methyl: Methyl group (CYP450 oxidation: hydroxymethyl → aldehyde → carboxylic acid)
analysis.admet.metabolism.phenol: Phenolic OH (prone to Phase II metabolism: glucuronidation/sulfation)
analysis.admet.toxicity.alkyl_halide: Alkyl halide → potential alkylating agent, depletes glutathione
analysis.admet.toxicity.aniline: Aromatic amine → may undergo N-oxidation to carcinogenic N-hydroxy metabolites
analysis.admet.toxicity.epoxide: Epoxide → highly reactive, can covalently bind DNA/proteins, potentially carcinogenic
analysis.admet.toxicity.halogen: Halogen substituent → metabolically stable, but may bioaccumulate
analysis.admet.toxicity.michael: Michael acceptor (α,β-unsaturated carbonyl) → non-specific nucleophile binding, potential hepatotoxicity/skin sensitization
analysis.admet.toxicity.nitro: Nitroaromatic → may be reduced by CYP450 to toxic nitroso/hydroxylamine intermediates, mutagenic risk
analysis.admet.toxicity.none: No common structural alerts detected, generally low toxicity risk
analysis.lipinski.good: Meets Lipinski's Rule of Five, good oral bioavailability potential
analysis.lipinski.violated: Violates {n} rule(s). Oral absorption may be limited, but many successful drugs also violate the rule of five.

footer.built_with: Built with Streamlit | ML: Random Forest + GNN | AI: Kimi (Moonshot AI) | DB: 100+ local + PubChem API
footer.subtitle: Solubility · pKa · Pharmacology | AI-Powered Chemistry Platform
header.subtitle: Predict Aqueous Solubility, pKa & Pharmacological Profiles from Molecular Structure
header.title: DisSolve
header.welcome_text: This app predicts aqueous solubility (logS), acid-base behavior (pKa), and pharmacological profiles from molecular structure using a Machine Learning model trained on 11,000+ organic compounds. Explore 2D & 3D molecular structures, pKa chemistry insights, and AI-generated explanations.

result.ai.need_manual: AI explanation requires manual invocation (API credits consumed)
result.ai.title: AI Chemistry Explanation
result.display_error: Parsing failed during display. Please re-enter SMILES.
result.gnn.desc: GNNExplainer identifies the bonds and atom features most important for this molecule's solubility prediction. Warmer colors = higher importance.
result.gnn.how_to_read_desc: GNNExplainer identifies which bonds and atom features contribute most to the solubility prediction by analyzing message-passing paths. High-importance (warm-colored) bonds are key structure-property relationship sites.
result.gnn.how_to_read_html:
GNNExplainer explains model predictions by learning "importance weights" for each chemical bond:

- Warm-colored edges = the model considers these bonds and their atoms most important for solubility prediction
- Cool/dim edges = these bonds have less impact on the prediction

How it works:
1. Each edge (bond) starts with a learnable weight (0~1)
2. Forward pass uses the weighted graph, keeping only high-weight edges
3. Optimization target: "weighted prediction ≈ original prediction" while keeping minimal edges
4. Final weight = importance of that edge to the model's prediction

Note:
- GNNExplainer is an approximation; slight variations across runs are normal
- High importance ≠ the bond is more chemically reactive
- Combine with SHAP (RF mode) and pKa factor decomposition for full interpretation
result.gnn.how_to_read_tech:
**Atom Feature Importance** shows which dimensions of the 37-dim atom feature vector matter most:
- Atom type (C, N, O, or other)
- Degree (how many atoms it's connected to)
- Hybridization (sp², sp³)
- Aromaticity, chirality, ring membership
result.gnn.model_unavailable: GNN explanation failed (model not loaded or molecule invalid)
result.gnn.no_bonds: This molecule has no non-hydrogen bonds, cannot analyze bond importance
result.gnn.title: GNN Explainability — Atom/Bond Attention Analysis
result.ood.high.desc: This molecule deviates significantly from the training data distribution. Prediction may be highly unreliable — use only as a qualitative reference.
result.ood.high.title: **Out-of-Distribution Warning — Prediction may be unreliable**
result.ood.low: In-Distribution — This molecule falls within the training data chemical space. Prediction is reliable.
result.ood.medium.desc: This molecule partially deviates from the training data distribution. Results may have notable error. Interpret with caution.
result.ood.medium.title: **Out-of-Distribution Notice — Prediction may have some error**
result.pharma.admet.absorption_caption: **Absorption** determines the efficiency of drug entry from the administration site into the bloodstream. Key factors: polarity (TPSA), lipophilicity (LogP), ionization state (pKa vs pH), H-bond capacity, and molecular size. Oral absorption primarily occurs in the small intestine (large surface area, rich blood flow); some acidic drugs begin absorption in the stomach.
result.pharma.admet.distribution_caption:
**Distribution** describes the movement of drugs from blood into tissues and organs.
- **Volume of Distribution (Vd)**: Higher Vd = drug favors tissue distribution; lower Vd = mainly stays in plasma
- **Plasma Protein Binding**: High binding = slow-release drug reservoir; low binding = more free drug, faster action and excretion
- **Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB)**: Low TPSA + moderate LogP favors CNS penetration
result.pharma.admet.excretion_caption:
**Excretion** is the process of eliminating drugs and their metabolites:
- **Renal excretion**: molecules with MW < 350 Da and moderate polarity are filtered through glomeruli into urine
- **Hepatobiliary excretion**: molecules with MW > 500 Da or highly lipophilic tend to be excreted via bile
- Tubular reabsorption can return lipophilic molecules to the blood, prolonging half-life
result.pharma.admet.metabolism_caption:
**Metabolism** primarily occurs in the liver in two phases:
- **Phase I** (oxidation/reduction/hydrolysis): catalyzed mainly by CYP450 enzymes, introducing or exposing polar groups
- **Phase II** (conjugation): attaches glucuronic acid, sulfate, amino acids, etc. to increase water solubility for excretion
- CYP3A4 metabolizes ~50% of clinical drugs; CYP2D6 has significant genetic polymorphism
result.pharma.admet.toxicity_caption: **Toxicity** assessment is based on Structural Alerts — functional groups or substructures historically associated with toxicity: - This analysis is based on structural features only, not actual toxicity — toxicity depends on dose, metabolism, individual variation, etc. - Structural alerts are important drug design screening tools, but have many false positives — drugs with alert structures can still be safe - Actual toxicity requires Ames test, hERG assay, animal studies, and clinical trials for validation
result.pharma.analysis.amphoteric: **Amphoteric molecule**: complex ionization behavior across pH ranges; absorption site depends on specific structure. May require special formulations (e.g., enteric-coated tablets).
result.pharma.analysis.mid_acid: **Whole GI tract absorption**: moderate pKa, unionized fraction in both stomach and intestine. Note: high unionized fraction means strong lipophilicity, may irritate gastric mucosa.
result.pharma.analysis.strong_acid: **Gastric absorption advantage**: pKa < 4, mostly unionized in stomach acid (pH 1.5), lipophilic, easily absorbed by gastric mucosa. Examples: Aspirin (pKa 3.5), Ibuprofen (pKa 4.9).
result.pharma.analysis.strong_base: **Intestinal absorption primary**: strongly basic molecules are fully ionized in the stomach; absorption increases in the small intestine (pH 6.8). Examples: Metformin (pKa ~12.4).
result.pharma.analysis.weak_base: **Weak base**: slightly ionized in the stomach, well absorbed in the small intestine. May partially ionize in blood (pH 7.4), increasing water solubility for renal excretion.
result.pharma.fsp3_detail: sp³ carbons: {n_sp3}/{n_carbon} | Higher sp³ fraction improves solubility & 3D diversity
result.pharma.fsp3_note: Fsp³ reflects molecular 3D character / saturation. Fsp³ ≥ 0.45 is generally associated with better clinical candidate properties.
result.pharma.ionization_chart_title: Unionized fraction across physiological pH | pKa = {val:.2f}
result.pharma.linkage.combo_acceptable: **Summary**: High solubility compensates for poor gastric absorption, good absorption in small intestine, acceptable bioavailability.
result.pharma.linkage.combo_challenging: **Summary**: Low solubility + strong base = **dual challenge for oral absorption**, may require enteric coating or injection.
result.pharma.linkage.combo_good: **Summary**: High solubility + gastric absorption = **excellent oral bioavailability**, suitable for standard tablets.
result.pharma.linkage.not_loaded: pKa model not loaded. Pharmacological analysis unavailable.
result.pharma.linkage.pka_mid_acid: **pKa**: Moderate acid (pKa={val:.1f}), absorbed throughout GI tract, no strict timing requirement.
result.pharma.linkage.pka_neutral: **pKa**: Near neutral (pKa={val:.1f}), absorption behavior is complex.
result.pharma.linkage.pka_strong_base: **pKa**: Strong base (pKa={val:.1f}), poor gastric absorption, take **after meals**, mainly absorbed in small intestine.
result.pharma.linkage.pka_weak_acid: **pKa**: Weak acid (pKa={val:.1f}), good gastric absorption, best taken **on an empty stomach**.
result.pharma.linkage.pka_weak_base: **pKa**: Weak base (pKa={val:.1f}), mainly absorbed in small intestine, favors excretion in blood.
result.pharma.linkage.title: Solubility × pKa Joint Analysis
result.pharma.lipinski_history: Lipinski's Rule of Five was proposed by Christopher Lipinski in 1997 to evaluate oral drug-likeness of compounds. It remains one of the most widely used ADME/drug-likeness pre-screening filters in medicinal chemistry.
result.pharma.lipinski_history_html:
• Christopher Lipinski (Pfizer, 1997) analyzed 2,245 drug candidates that reached Phase II clinical trials, summarizing 5 empirical rules for oral drugs
• Molecules with ≤1 violation are more likely to have good oral absorption and bioavailability
• This is a screening guideline, not an absolute rule — many successful drugs violate it (e.g., natural products, antibiotics, anticancer drugs)
• Beyond Ro5 (bRo5) molecules are an important direction in modern medicinal chemistry (e.g., PROTACs, macrocycles)
result.pharma.pka_acid_low: **pKa**: Weak acid (pKa={val:.1f}), good gastric absorption, best taken **on an empty stomach**.
result.pharma.pka_acid_mid: **pKa**: Moderate acid (pKa={val:.1f}), absorbed throughout the GI tract. No strict timing requirement.
result.pharma.pka_base_high: **pKa**: Strong base (pKa={val:.1f}), poor gastric absorption. Take **after meals** to reduce gastric irritation.
result.pharma.pka_base_low: **pKa**: Weak base (pKa={val:.1f}), primarily absorbed in the small intestine. Excretion favored in blood.
result.pharma.qed_note: QED integrates 8 descriptors including MW, LogP, H-bonds, and charge. Values closer to 1 indicate higher drug-likeness.
result.pharma.sascore_note: SAscore estimates synthetic difficulty based on fragment frequency & complexity. 1 = very easy, 10 = very hard.
result.pka.factor_guide: **How to read this chart**: Longer purple bars = factor promotes **proton donation/acceptance**; longer cyan bars = factor **resists** proton transfer. Unlike SHAP, these are not ML weights but **real structural chemistry effects**.
result.pka.glossary_hybrid: **Hybridization / Aromaticity** — additional stability from sp² carbon ratio and aromatic conjugation
result.pka.glossary_inductive: **Inductive Effect** — electronegative atoms attract or repel electrons through σ bonds, affecting proton binding and release
result.pka.glossary_intra_hb: **Intramolecular H-Bond** — H-bond between groups in the same molecule, shielding polarity and modulating pKa
result.pka.glossary_resonance: **Resonance / Conjugation** — π electrons delocalize in conjugated systems, stabilizing ionized forms
result.pka.glossary_steric: **Steric Hindrance** — bulky atoms/groups hinder proton approach and departure, altering reactivity
result.pka.how_to_read_desc: Purple bars show factors that enhance acidity (electron-withdrawing / resonance stabilization). Cyan bars show factors that enhance basicity (electron-donating).
result.pka.model_unavailable: pKa model not loaded. pKa analysis unavailable.
result.pka.model_unavailable_short: pKa model not loaded. pKa analysis unavailable.
result.solubility.insight_hbond: **H-Bond Donors/Acceptors** tell us how well the molecule can form hydrogen bonds with water.
result.solubility.insight_logp: **LogP** measures lipophilicity. Lower LogP means the molecule prefers water over oil.
result.solubility.insight_tpsa: **TPSA** (Topological Polar Surface Area) measures how much of the molecule is polar. Higher TPSA usually means better water solubility.
result.solubility.notable_disagree: 📊 RF & GNN notably disagree (|Δ|={diff:.2f}). Ensemble favors GNN.
result.solubility.severe_disagree: ⚠️ RF & GNN severely disagree (|Δ|={diff:.2f}). Using GNN. Interpret with caution.
result.solubility.shap_guide: SHAP values show the contribution of each molecular feature to the solubility prediction. Red = increases solubility, blue = decreases solubility.
result.solubility.shap_insight_generic: LogP is the most important feature for solubility, followed by TPSA and H-bond counts. If LogP pushes toward insolubility but TPSA pushes toward solubility, the molecule has a hydrophilic-hydrophobic competition.
result.solubility.shap_insight_leading: **Key Analysis**: The model predicts this molecule is **{level}** (logS = {logS:.3f}).
result.solubility.shap_resisting: However, the following factors resist this trend, pushing the molecule {target}: {factors}.
result.solubility.shap_shift: Compared to the training set average (base {base:.3f}), the molecule's structural features shifted the prediction {direction} by {shift:.3f} units.
